content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

June 15, 1992


Stella Matthews
Campaign Reporting Section
Registrar-Recorder/County Clerk
5557 Ferguson Drive
P.O. Box 30450
Los Angeles, CA  90030-0450

RE:  Your Request for Confirmation
     of Telephone Advice
     Our File No. A-92-309

Dear Ms. Matthews:

     You have requested confirmation of advice provided to you
regarding the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").1/

QUESTION

     What are the filing requirements for candidates who ran in
the April 7, 1992, special election?

CONCLUSION

     Candidates who did not have a controlled committee and who
did not anticipate raising or spending $1,000 or more during the
calendar year were required to file the Form 470, Officeholder and
Candidate Campaign Statement-Short Form by February 27, 1992, the
deadline for filing the first pre-election statement in connection
with the April 7, 1992, election.  Candidates who anticipated
raising or spending $1,000 or more in connection with this
election were required to file the Form 490, Officeholder,
Candidate, and Controlled Committee Campaign Statement--Long Form,
whether or not the candidates qualified as a committee by the
filing deadline for the first pre-election statement (February 27,
1992, covering the period January 1 through February 22, 1992).
The deadline for filing the second pre-election statement was
March 26, 1992, covering the period February 23 through March 21,

_______________
1/ Government Code Section 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804-0807 ● (916)322-5660

Stella Matthews
Our File No. A-92-309
Page 2


1992. Candidates who fail to file campaign statements by the
filing deadlines are subject to late fines.

FACTS

A candidate involved in the April 7, 1992, special election
paid a filing fee of $525 on February 20, 1992. The candidate
filed a copy of the Statement of Organization, Form 410, with the
Registrar's office indicating the candidate qualified as a
committee on February 25, 1992. The candidate failed to file the
first pre-election campaign statement which was due February 27,
1992, covering the period January 1, 1992 through February 22,
1992. The Registrar's office notified the candidate on March 9,
1992, of her failure to file the first pre-election statement. On
March 27, 1992, the candidate filed a copy of the Form 490 with
the Registrar's office covering the period January 1, 1992 through
March 21, 1992, combining the first and second pre-election
periods. The original of the Form 490, covering this same period,
was filed with the Secretary of State's office. On May 1, 1992,
the Commission notified you of the Secretary of State's decision
to accept the statement as filed and not require the candidate to
amend the period covered and file two separate reports. You state
in your letter that during our telephone conversation, I indicated
the Registrar's ability to assess a late fine for the first pre-
election statement required to be filed by this candidate was
nullified due to the fact that the Secretary of State accepted the
Form 490 as filed. During several of our conversations, I recall
informing you that the Secretary of State's willingness to accept
this candidate's Form 490, covering the period January 1 through
March 21, 1992, would not prevent or prohibit the Los Angeles
Registrar's office from assessing a late fine on the copy of the
campaign statement which was required to be filed with you by
February 27 covering the period January 1 through February 22,
1992.

ANALYSIS

The Act defines a candidate as:

An individual who is listed on the ballot...
or who receives a contribution or makes an
expenditure or gives his consent for any

Stella Matthews
Our File No. A-92-309
Page 3


other person to receive a contribution or make
an expenditure with a view to bringing about
his nomination or election to any elective office,
whether or not the specific elective office for
which he will seek nomination or election is known
at the time the contribution is received or the
expenditure is made and whether or not he has
announced his candidacy or filed a declaration of
candidacy at such time... (Emphasis added.)

(Section 82007.)

During a six-month period when an election is held,
candidates and their controlled committees must file pre-election
campaign statements in addition to the semi-annual statement.
(Sections 84200, 84200.5, 85200.7, and 84200.8.) Candidates who
do not have a committee, and who do not anticipate raising or
spending $1,000 or more during the entire calendar year, may file
one short form campaign statement (Form 470) in connection with an
election and incur no additional filing requirements as long as
receipts and expenditures remain under $1,000. (Section 84206.)

Any person who files a campaign statement or report after the
deadline imposed by the Act is liable for a late fine. Section
91013 provides:

(a) If any person files an original statement
or report after any deadline imposed by this
act, he or she shall, in addition to any other
penalties or remedies established by this act,
be liable in the amount of ten dollars ($10)
per day after the deadline until the statement
or report is filed, to the officer with whom
the statement or report is required to be filed...

(b) If any person files a copy of a statement or
report after any deadline imposed by this act, he
or she shall, in addition to any other penalties
or remedies established by this chapter, be liable
in the amount of ten dollars ($10) per day, starting
10 days or five days in the case of the second
preelection campaign statement, after the officer
has sent specific written notice of the filing
requirement and until the statement is filed....

There is nothing in the Act that sets out exceptions from
imposing late fines when a candidate fails to file his/her
campaign disclosure statement by the filing deadline. The Act

Stella Matthews
Our File No. A-92-309
Page 4


does provide that filing officers must exercise discretion when
assessing penalties and that the assessment of late fines must be
exercised on an impartial basis. As stated in the Facts above,
the Los Angeles Registrar's office is not prohibited from
assessing a late fine on the statement which was required to be
filed by February 27 covering the period January 1 through
February 22, 1992.

If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at 916/322-5662.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

[signature]
By: Mary Ann Kvasager
Political Reform Consultant
